title: refactor(test): POC integration tests
body:
<!--
  Thanks for submitting a pull request!
  Please provide enough information so that others can review your pull request.
-->

**Summary**

<!--
  Explain the **motivation** for making this change.
  What existing problem does the pull request solve?
  Are there any linked issues?
-->

Our current test suite is way too close to the implementation details and the actual widgets usage is not truthfully tested, making it harder for us to be confident in the test suite. Any change in the codebase can potentially break multiple tests even if we did not break anything in reality. And at the same time its "freezing" some projects details making internal changes more complicated than it should be.

IMHO it would benefit us to write less unit tests and more integration tests, closer on how our widgets and connectors are used by real users.

This PR is a POC to use [Testing Library](https://testing-library.com) on InstantSearch.js to write such integration tests.

For this POC I tested only one flavor but the same tools and principles can be applied on other flavors as well.

## Guiding principles

> The more your tests resemble the way your software is used, the more confidence they can give you. - Testing Library guiding principles

### Widgets

* **Usage**: The widget must be used the same way a user will use it. It means initializing an `instantsearch` instance, using the `addWidget` method then calling the `start` method).
* **Interactions**: Changes are only triggered using simulated user interactions (using [Testing Library events API](https://testing-library.com/docs/dom-testing-library/api-events).
* **Multiple widgets**: Multiple widgets can be initialized in a test to see if a particular widget interact correctly with another one.
* **Validation**: Results must only be tested by checking the widget output (DOM) or, in some cases, the potential side effects (API request).

### Connectors

* **Usage**: The connector must be used the same way a user will use it. It means calling `instantsearch.connectors.connect*` with a custom render function (a [Jest mock function](https://jestjs.io/docs/en/jest-object#jestfnimplementation) for our case).
* **Interactions**: Changes are only triggered using methods passed to the rendering function (captured using our [Jest mock function](https://jestjs.io/docs/en/jest-object#jestfnimplementation)).
* **Multiple widgets**: Multiple widgets can be initialized in a test to see if a particular widget interact correctly with another one.
* **Validation**: Results must only be tested by checking parameters passed to the rendering function or, in some cases, the potential side effects (API request).

Using theses principles we limit ourselves to use and interact with our widgets/connectors like a real user, limiting the needs for mocking and the risk to rely on internals for testing.

Resources:
 * [Testing Library](https://testing-library.com)
 * [jest-dom](https://github.com/testing-library/jest-dom)
 * [Kent C. Dodds – Write tests. Not too many. Mostly integration](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Fha2bVoC8SE)

**PS**: Other tests are failing since I removed the `preact-compat` -> `react` alias in the Babel configuration. Both test suites can coexists but I did not take the time to tweak the configuration for this POC.
